content.text: Context flows down the tree, but updates originate in child components. A child triggers a context update by calling the setter function that the Provider placed in the context value. The Provider owns the state; the children read and modify it through the context object. Understanding this flow — and how to make it efficient — is the core skill for context in practice.

attrs.alt: Child calls setTheme from context, re-rendering the Provider and all consumers

content.text: The Mechanism: Setter in the Context Value
The key insight: the Provider includes both the current value and a function to update it in its context object. Children destructure the setter from
and call it just like they would call a prop callback — but without needing the setter to be threaded through intermediate components.
, the state update triggers a re-render of the Provider component (the one that owns the
). The Provider passes the new value as its
prop, and React re-renders every component that called
with this context — including all siblings of the component that triggered the update.

) is stable across renders — React guarantees the same function reference for the lifetime of the component. Destructuring it from context and using it in an event handler is safe; it never becomes stale.

When the new value depends on the current value (toggle, increment, push to array), use the functional updater form. This avoids reading state from the closure and ensures the update is based on the latest value, even if multiple updates queue up.

If multiple quick updates queue (e.g., user clicks fast), React batches them. Each functional update receives the result of the previous one — preventing lost updates that would occur if all updates captured the same initial value from the closure.
For complex state with multiple fields and update patterns, replace
. The dispatch function is stable and can be included in the context value alongside the state object.
any child can trigger any state transition by name, without knowing the update logic. The reducer is the single source of truth for how state changes.
Splitting State and Dispatch Into Two Contexts

An advanced optimisation: separate the state object and the dispatch function into two separate contexts. Components that only dispatch (buttons, forms) subscribe only to the dispatch context and never re-render when state changes. Components that render state subscribe to the state context.
Now a button that only calls dispatch will never re-render when the state changes, because it is subscribed only to
Memoising the Context Value to Avoid Extra Re-renders
is stable, there is no stale closure problem. But if the context value object itself is re-created on every Provider render (without
), every consumer re-renders on every Provider render — even when the data is unchanged.
Single value or few independent fields

React DevTools lets you verify context updates: select the Provider component and inspect its state. When a child calls the setter, the Provider's state in the Hooks panel updates in real time. Select any consumer and confirm the context value changed in its context section.

React 18 automatically batches all state updates inside event handlers, even across multiple setters. Multiple dispatch calls or setState calls inside one event handler produce a single re-render, not one per call.

Before React 18, batching only occurred inside React event handlers. In React 18, it applies everywhere — including inside Promises and setTimeout. Multiple context updates issued asynchronously are also batched.
When an action involves a server call, update the context immediately (optimistic) and roll back on error. The setter is synchronous — optimistic UI is straightforward.
Four patterns that cause subtle bugs when updating context from children:
Reading state from the closure in an event handler instead of using the functional updater — can miss concurrent updates.
Not memoising the Provider value — every render re-creates the object, causing all consumers to re-render even when data hasn't changed.
Placing state that is only used by one part of the tree in a top-level context — causes global re-renders for local changes. Keep state as local as possible.
Dispatching inside a useEffect without listing the dispatch function as a dependency — dispatch is stable, so this is technically harmless, but listing it avoids lint warnings and makes intent clear.
A shopping cart is a canonical context use case: multiple components (product cards, cart icon, checkout) all read and update the same cart state.
Compute totals and counts from the reducer state during render (
) rather than storing them in state. They are always up to date and require no extra action.
Tests for context updates verify that: (1) the initial state is correct, (2) calling the update function changes the rendered output, and (3) the change propagates to all consumers.

Children update context by calling the setter (or dispatch function) that the Provider includes in the context value. The Provider owns the state; children trigger changes through the functions it exposes.
include setField alongside field in the Provider's value object.
useState setters are always stable — safe to use in callbacks without becoming stale.
use setField(prev => ...) when the new value depends on the current one.
preferable when the state has multiple fields with named transitions.
separate state and dispatch contexts so dispatch-only consumers never re-render on state changes.
always memoize the value object to prevent re-rendering all consumers on unrelated Provider renders.
